narrative:
I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the admissions model predicted a 73% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your law school application. This prediction is based on your academic record, test scores, background, and other details compared to thousands of past students. You ranked in the bottom decile for your grades in both your first and third years of school, far below the typical student's mid-range position around the fifth or sixth decile. Your LSAT score of 27 is also notably lower than the average of about 37 that the model sees in successful applicants. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.3 is a bit stronger than the usual 3.2, and your upper-middle family income is solid, these weren't enough to offset the concerns.

The model's decision was influenced most strongly by your third-year decile rank, which pushed the prediction heavily toward expecting bar failure. Right behind that was your first-year decile rank, also contributing significantly in the same direction. Your LSAT score was the next biggest factor pulling toward failure, followed by your family income bracket, racial background as Asian (less common at about 6% of students versus 84% White), and undergraduate GPA. Even your choices like being female (43% of students) and planning full-time enrollment (typical at 93%) played smaller roles, all nudging slightly toward the failure prediction. In essence, your early and mid-school performance stood out as the top concerns when stacked against students who typically pass the bar.

Compared to peers who succeeded, your low decile ranks and LSAT suggest challenges in core academic preparation that the model flags as high-risk for the bar. To improve your future chances, focus on what you can realistically address: consider retaking the LSAT to boost that critical score closer to or above the average, as it's a key lever the model weighs heavily. Strengthening any upcoming grades or academic deciles through targeted study habits could help rebuild that foundation too. While background factors like income, gender, race, and enrollment status are harder to change, excelling in modifiable areas like test prep and performance has turned predictions around for others in similar spots. With dedication, you can shift the odds—keep pursuing your goals, and reach out to advisors for personalized bar prep strategies.
